agent_raw: The fraud model benchmark comparison has two critical flaws that prevent the PR-AUC difference from being interpreted as model quality evidence. First, the baseline was evaluated using 30-day provisional fraud labels while the challenger used 90-day matured labels. These are different ground-truth targets: 90-day labels include additional chargebacks and resolved disputes that the 30-day labels miss. A model evaluated against a more complete target will naturally show higher PR-AUC than one evaluated against an incomplete target, regardless of underlying model quality. The six-basis-point difference may be entirely explained by label maturation, not model improvement. Second, the evaluation periods differ: Q1 for the baseline and Q2 for the challenger. Between Q1 and Q2, a merchant onboarding campaign changed the transaction mix and geographic distribution. This population shift means the two models faced different data distributions, confounding any metric comparison. The temporal split design (by week, forward-looking) is correct and not the issue. The required fix is to re-evaluate both models on the same quarter with the same 90-day label maturation and report a paired bootstrap confidence interval for the PR-AUC difference. Without this, the migration decision has no valid evidentiary basis.
all_issues_raised: Label maturation mismatch: 30-day baseline vs. 90-day challenger labels
Different evaluation quarters with intervening merchant onboarding campaign
No confidence interval for small PR-AUC delta at rare fraud prevalence
